speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wouldn't call it a surprise. It was-- it was certainly a, a, a bad omen. We realized that, you know, the-- that's the beginning of the end. We, we, we knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when the first German tanks rolled into Ujpest, where I lived at the time, to, to Kiraly Street. And the, the local population received them warmly, the majority have. There were people-- this was very interesting from then on-- what was the local population's reaction. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most people whose fortunes were attached to, to the ruling regime and, therefore, to Germany, naturally were barracking for Germany, right, because that was resulting in Hungary recovering some of the lost territory. And their livelihood, you know, depended on that. And so these people were barracking for, for the German and, by that time, the official Hungarian line. I must admit that the official Hungarian line-- and if you read that book or part of it, you will see it very clearly from it-- varied in their degree of support to Germany. I mean, by that time we were occupied, by the-- Hungary was occupied by Germany. And it was no option but to toe the German line. But because of the realization that it is-- by no means is it a foregone conclusion that Germany will won the war-- will win the war, some people in the governing circle started to look for options, right, for, for alternatives. However, speaking of the-- of the-- of the general public, which is-- that is interesting, the majority of that, as I said, were toeing the German line. And if it was at the expense of the Jews, so it-- so it be, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your-- what was the situation-- I'm interested in the atmosphere in your neighborhood and what the situation was like that you were living in. I mean, I take it if your father's business had had to close, how were you doing financially? And how did you live from day to day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we had some modest accumulated reserves, right? But there was no income coming in at the time. We had to pay 100 pengo for our rent, which was a, a very big obligation each month. To give you an idea, 200 pengo was the ideal monthly income, right? There were songs made out of that, that whoever earns 200 pengo can, can joke and live quite easily, right? Out of this 200 pengo, which a modest-- which a family modestly required, we paid $100 for our rent. And the other $100, which my mother very [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] frugally has converted into food and other issues, issues, socks, occasional clothing line.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the, the, the general population welcomed, welcomed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hitler-- the, the German occupation. There were-- there were very decent-- very decent Hungarians-- I would like to talk to you about it later on-- who, not necessarily by foreseeing what is going to happen and wanting to cover their, their official back, but-- through humanitarian reasons, right, because there were-- not too many, but there were who have-- you know, they look through the, the blind when Jews have been taken away, said to their wives, that that's horrible, isn't it? That, that shouldn't happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them, however, said that's good. Tomorrow I get that, that house next door, right, which is the most natural, human, greedy reaction. Because humans are greedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were people who, who, who, who were in a disagreement. Very few people have been, however, able and willing to do something about it because that was very dangerous. If you were openly helping a Jew or hiding a Jew, then you finished up in Auschwitz yourself, right? So I understand their, their reluctance, right, to become heroes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the general Hungarian [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] reaction by the-- by the-- by the populace.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Ujpest, too? It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, I, I have to-- I have to-- I have to perhaps go in a more chronological order because then I come back to, to, to your question, right? So the Germans came in, right, in-- on the 19th of March, '44. From then on, events started to roll very quickly, right? It was all organized. The Germans knew exactly what they have done in, in Belgium and Holland and France and Austria. It was a-- it was a module they have to put in place, and which they did very, very well. We didn't know that, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your impressions at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My impressions of the time? Things, events were, were happening so quickly that I didn't know where to turn, right? I had no time to, to, to, to develop impressions. My personal impression, my instinctive impression was that there is going to be-- we'll have a horrible end. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so we had to wear yellow stars. That was the first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] obligation we had, which, as I said, was a, a well-rehearsed, well-proven method of going about this systematic extermination of Jews, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was-- it was all well-- as, as well done, as well-rehearsed as, as, as building a, a complicated building, which has got ingredients. And you just put these ingredients in place. And then everything just fall into place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your yellow star come from? Did you have to make it yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to make it ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my mother made it. So we have to wear the yellow star, which, which, which has-- which has segregated ourselves, which marked us, right, which made us distinguishable from anybody else. It was the first step.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember wearing it the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I do. I was very-- was very ashamed. I was very-- I was-- I was dejected.

speaker: interviewer
text: And seeing other Jewish people wearing them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there role models-- and I know I digress, but were there role models in the Jewish community, people who were figures who stood out in one way or another, the rabbis or the leaders? Can you remember figures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I do. We had Denes Friedman. Friedman Denes was our chief rabbi in Ujpest. Ujpest had a Jewish population of 20,000. It was a very sizable population. Friedman Denes was a tall, impressive man, unbelievably articulate in Hungarian, a, a very clever man. He taught-- he taught us philosophy-- I think I told you that-- in, in, in in the school. Because the religious rabbis had access to school--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: --education. Yes, in the gymnasium. And he taught philosophy. He didn't teach us religious. We had another teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- however, if I remember Denes Friedman-- Friedman Denes, I respect him more for his intellectual qualities, not so much as his, his ability of communal, emotional leadership. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He, he didn't show-- admittedly, my, my father was not his best friend, right? It was 20,000 Jews there, right? My father was not part of the-- of the board of directors of the-- of the Jewish community. So I have no direct recollection of his behavior-- Friedman Denes's behavior. But my general impression of that time was that while, you know, the, the sermon he delivered was impeccable and, at times, very moving, but he lacked in leadership action, right, which would have-- which would have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] enthused them-- the, the, the Jewish people there to, to do certain things or not to do certain things, to go away.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was not a-- he was not a, a leader in this sense. And, in fact, it was no leader in this sense because there were no such personalities and mainly because the circumstances were not, not-- have not been allowing for such personalities to develop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm coming back to, when you were in a straitjacket, then you just sit there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's nothing you can do. And when you are gagged, what, what do you do? You just sit there.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, So to return to your chronology, you said that one of the first things that happened was the wearing of the Jewish star.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were wearing of the Jewish stars. And then they started to-- they started again, a very well-proven method of the past, what they used in other areas-- to concentrate us in, in confined areas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you exactly what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was-- I was called up on the 15th of May, 1944-- being a 19-year-old or 19-and-1/2-year-old Jewish boy-- into a-- into a, a, a labor camp, which was the alternative of, of military duties for a Jewish boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been expecting to be called up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been expecting that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, we know that we will be called up. If we had not been Jewish, we would have been called up, in any case, in the military service. So why should the Jewish boys be, be spared? It was very interesting that, by that time, instructions started to appear for, for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] moving out and relocating ourselves in a-- in a-- the term-- in a pre-determined, confined area, which became the ghetto later on. That worried me no end because, I mean, my poor mother looked after our, our very little flat so impeccably. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You know, and the thought of, of-- well, we lived there, by that time, 20 years, right, should be-- should be left. And, of course, we had this very typical, typical bourgeois attitude. We didn't have a great deal of other assets, but that piece of furniture that, you know, was, was was all-important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for that to have a, a blemish on it, that was unheard of, right, not speaking of the fact that you might have to leave it there. You won't be able to take it with you. That was the, the mentality there, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they called me up-- and by that time, I knew exactly that the, the ghettoization will happen. That filled me with tremendous concern. Because I knew that they will not be able to do it physically, you know, physically-- I mean, the sheer logistics of it, right, to move those things. And, and not knowing where they are going to go, whom they are going to live with filled me with, with considerable worry and concern. However, there's nothing I could do about it. I was-- I left on the 15th of May. And I knew that, you know, that was the last. [CRYING] And, [CRYING] and I was called up to Jolsva, actually. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Jolsva is a-- is a place-- [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] is a-- is a place in Hungary, in northern Hungary, where the Jewish boys concentration occurred. I remember we, we, we traveled together with my friends, you know. We were all called up at this, this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] age group. And, and we arrived in Jolsva, which is on the northern part of Hungary, at night, I remember. And they waited for us. They kicked us out from the wagon-- we arrived. And that's where the military atmosphere-- a Jewish-labor-camp, military atmosphere started. We didn't know at the time that, of course, this labor camp thing is our savior. Because those who, who, who stayed at home-- and I will give you an example for that-- have been deported with their parents. And they didn't come back. We had one, one colleague there-- I'm sorry, I left out from the eight, one, one person, Spitzer Yuri-- because it was only seven-- who also perished. He was deported with the parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this Spitzer Yuri, he had a weak heart, right, was looking forward to, to, to-- not to be accepted in the-- in the-- in the labor camp. And he managed that with certificates. And I remember the time after two, three days there, we had a, a physical examination.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was-- he was sent back. And, you know, that was a tremendous-- we thought-- a tremendous achievement. And we sent messages through him back to, to Ujpest, by which time-- because the last few days have already started the, the movement there-- the movement to the new, allocated ghetto area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your parents moved after you had already--

speaker: interviewee
text: After I left. When I-- I left my parents in the old place there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive any messages from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have received letters from them. We send letters back to them. My mother wrote mainly-- I mean, my mother was, you know, he-- he would-- she would never say anything which would sadden me, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So whatever she said was censored [CRYING] by, by herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and so we, we-- in Jolsva, we, we, we have been allocated different, different tasks. I was young. I was very strong. I was-- I was physically at the peak of my-- of my, my ability.

speaker: interviewer
text: During my, my one and a half years as a-- as a-- in the trade certainly toughened me considerably, you know. I was much tougher than I was one and a half year ago when I was sitting in the school benches. My hands were like, like that of a worker, right? And, and most importantly, by that time, I was completely determined to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Jolsva, we went-- my first job was-- we were-- we were given different quarters to stay, which was mainly in a big, big barn in a-- in a country area, very primitive conditions. But it was summer at that time. It was June by the time. It was no major problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was reasonable. It was not Maxim. But, you know, we-- it was in a country. It was ample food around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And morale amongst, amongst the young men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Morale was good. It was quite good. We had a camaraderie. And what is interesting that in our battalion, it was six boys from Ujpest. And we really stuck together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in a-- in a fragmented group of-- in a fragmented group of a hundred people, if six people really stick together, the cohesion of the six people will carry the day. And that's what happened. Because it happened-- I mean, as much as it was within our scope, right? It happened what, what we wanted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my first allocation was in, in the furnace. We-- from Jolsva, we went to Ozd, O-Z-D, which was an industrial city in Hungary, one of the few cities, if not the only city, where Hungary produced iron-- iron ore-- iron out of iron ore. And we went there. And there was a big furnace. And my job was-- in this big hole in front of the furnace, I was given a big-- a big stick-- you know, it was 6 meter long-- that when a sign was given, I had to poke into this furnace. And then the, the molten, molten iron sort of came out. And this big hole where sand forms were, this flowed in. And, and that's when it consolidated, eventually. Then we were given hoses to hose it, so that we encouraged a quick solidification of, of this molten lava. And, and then we were given big, big hammers. We, we broke it up. And then we had to carry it in front of the big hole-- it was on a railway line. A big wagon was waiting. And we dropped it in. And then it was carted away.

speaker: interviewer
text: It sounds like hard work. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard work, absolutely hard. The heat was unbearable. We haven't been given any protective clothing whatsoever. Gloves, but apart from that, certainly no modern version of it. And it was really hard work. It was summer. It was hot. But, look, we survived. It was-- you know, I, I, I worked there a few weeks. And, as I said earlier, the food was reasonable. It was one or two fatalities. People who, who fainted fell into the lava. And that was the end of them, of course. But apart from that, we survived. After a few weeks, we have been transferred to Arlo, A-R-L-O, which is another close-lying country area, where we have been felling wood-- timber. And, and we, we have been allocated a certain, certain [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] production minimum. We have-- had to do it. And others had to carry it down to the road from the hillside, where, where big, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three-, four-ton trucks trucked it away. My duty there was there this, this, this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] carrying it down to the-- to the-- to the road. And I remember we came-- it was-- it was quite, quite, I mean, very, very hard work, but quite OK. In the morning, we came there. And, I remember, the guard would say, so you two, you four people here, well, you have to do that, that, and that-- these big hunks of things have been already there. And we tried to get, get out of it by saying, oh, well, we can't do that over that, that, and that. And then we agreed that we do half of that and that. That's-- it was quite all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards were Hungarian soldiers. Some of them were, were country teachers and of this background. But we have been directly responsible to, to Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-betweens, right? I remember it was a fellow by the name of Fried Gezer. He was a-- I mean, we were 19 at the time. He was 36 at the time, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Very strange, very, very coincidentally, some of these people came from Odz. And they have, more or less, witnessed the deportation of the Jews from Odz there, in their own family, right? It was a very sad sight. Did they speak about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. They were-- for days, they have been heartbroken and unapproachable. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so we were responsible to him as such. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Pardon me. And, and it was very hard work. I mean, what we have done is we have lift those big trunks of cut trees, which are already cut into pieces. We had to lift it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had to drop it in such a way, as we learned it, that if you drop it like that [POINTS] it, it finds its way easier down to, to the road. And therefore, we have-- could do our assigned work much quicker and much easier, right? So we developed skills in that. And sometimes we finished our work at 2 o'clock. And then we sat down, and we-- then we waited there until, until it was time to go home, right? Because it was a negotiated lot each, each day. Right. And in the midst of this hard, physical labor, was there time and space to discuss other things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, our, our main discussion at the time centered around our family at home. Have you got a letter? What did they say? Because they were still writing at this time. It must have been in June because in July they were deported, on the 2nd of July. It was centering around survival, you know. That was the major topic. The major topic is that you had another chunk of meat in the soup, and-- you know, these, these simple day-to-day, hour-to-hour, minute-to-minute existence-- survival issues.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived a, a very-- if I think back-- a very egotistic, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] inward-looking life, you know, which essentially centered around survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was food one of the biggest concerns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food was one of the biggest concern. And we had food. I cannot say that we didn't have food, not particularly good quality. But we could buy a, a bit of cheese from the country people there. And we did not starve.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you describe maybe one day where you slept? How you got up? At work? Where you took your lunch? Do you remember a, a day, the routine of a day? Yes, well, we-- in Arlo, where, where, you know, where this, this timber work was done by us, we lived in a-- in a-- in a peasant house, which, at the back of it was a-- it would have been-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what do you call it in English, where the animals live?

speaker: interviewer
text: A barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where the animals sleep, particularly-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a barn, let's call it a barn, right? We lived, lived, lived in this barn. It was a, a, a clay floor. We put-- we put straw on that, right? And we made this comfortable as we could. And in this barn, I remember, we, we had-- we, we would have had-- the particular barn I was, because there was a group of them-- it would have been, I imagine, 15 people that there was. I remember a little other entrance to it. So it would be about 30 people in one barn, very narrow place allotted to each person. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And being in the country, it was no, no, no electric light there. It was very primitive, of course. And-- but it was summer. It was daylight fairly long. So we went to bed very early. I think we had to be in bed by 8:00, 8:00 or 8:30, certainly by 9 o'clock. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any books at all? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It was no-- you know, it's really strange, Danny, when you are in these circumstances, your intellectual requirement is, is, is, is diminished to, to zero. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- to my recollection, it was very, very little in this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was mainly, mainly keeping you alive, keeping yourself clean, filling up your stomach, talking about, about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the world things. We were very interested what was happening in the world, right? Political and military dispatches were always tremendously-- interest to us. And And how did they come to you? It must have been very, very isolated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, look, we always had this-- I mean, the, the source of true information was the BBC, the, the British Broadcasting Corporation, who was the only source at the time who could provide us the occasional news. And they were-- we did not listen to it ourselves because we, we had no radio and we had no opportunity of even-- we didn't dare to do that. However, we did have rumors circulating. And they are usually centered around what other people who had access to the would have heard the night before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So where there particular people who were often in the know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Fried Gezer, for example, our, our leader there, right, had such access, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he very discreetly would have told us, right-- but very discreetly, it wasn't an open discussion-- that you know, did you hear that Voronezh fell last night, you know? And that was tremendous. Because we are acutely interested in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were very knowledgeable, as far as the geography is concerned, right? We knew exactly where Voronezh is. And we could relate it to the-- we could draw military conclusions from it, right? Hmm. All right, so from the timber work that you did and-- I've forgotten the name already in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Arlo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Arlo. How long were you there for, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there-- [SIGHS] we were there, I would say-- [SIGHS] I would say, we would have been there until late July, early August--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I would say. Meanwhile, as I said earlier, they deported my parents, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive news of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I received news from that, but in an unreliable manner. We didn't receive news immediately. You must understand that Hitler managed to keep things so much confused, by which I understand-- by, by which I mean that my parents, by that time, had been moved two or three times, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Whether this last deportation move was the last one, we didn't know, right? We knew that they had been moved in a major way because it involved the railway, right, not just moving from one part of the city to another part of the city. But, but whether was a major move and whether that was to Auschwitz, this we didn't know-- Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because, because, because they made sure that nobody was informed, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: So you understand, therefore, when I say that to say at this stage that, yes, I knew that my parents have been deported on the 2nd of July to Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that is not a true statement.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, leave it there for the moment.
